metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile the '~29 test patch strings' metric in parent AC4 with dfb9's corrected taxonomy (zero string patches; ~18 path-constant files + ~18 dotted-import files to rewrite); update the parent AC to use the correct units.
evidence: Parent AC4: 'all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims (S5)'
dfb9 Approach: 'the first draft's ~29 mock.patch strings was wrong; there are zero mock.patch("rebar_reconciler.acli…") string patches'
dfb9 reclassifies into categories 1 (~23 object-level, unaffected), 2 (~18 path constants), 3 (~18 dotted imports)
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: Parent AC4 states 'all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims' as a measurable deliverable, but child dfb9 explicitly states the '~29 mock.patch strings' count is wrong — there are zero mock.patch string patches — and reclassifies the test surface into three categories with different counts (~23 object-level patches unaffected, ~18 path-constant files, ~18 dotted-import files). The parent metric cannot be satisfied as written because the child contradicts its own unit of measure.
findings.location: child dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9, Acceptance Criteria AC4 of parent epic, and dfb9 Testing/Scope sections
scenarios: A completion verifier checks for '~29 migrated test patch strings' and cannot reconcile that metric against dfb9's zero-string-patch taxonomy
A reviewer approving dfb9 disagrees on whether the AC is satisfied because the parent and child use incompatible units
findings.suggested_fix: Update parent AC4 to read: 'the category-2 (~18 path-constant) and category-3 (~18 dotted-import) test targets are rewritten to adapters.jira.* paths atomically with no re-export shims — verified by grep returning zero hits for old root paths (excluding path-agnostic patch.object sites).'

findings.finding: Parent AC-4 states 'all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims (S5)' but S5 (dfb9) explicitly contradicts this, saying 'the first draft's ~29 mock.patch strings was wrong; there are zero mock.patch string patches' — replacing the concept with categories 2 (~18 path-constant files) and 3 (~18 dotted-import files). The parent AC uses erroneous terminology that S5 does not reconcile upward.
findings.location: child dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9 — Context/Problem ('characterized correctly — the first draft's ~29 mock.patch strings was wrong'); parent Acceptance Criteria AC-4
findings.suggested_fix: In parent AC-4, replace 'all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims' with 'the category-2 (~18 path-constant) and category-3 (~18 dotted-import) test references are rewritten to adapters.jira.* paths (zero mock.patch string patches exist; the ~23 patch.object _load_acli tests are path-agnostic and verified still-green), with no re-export shims — per S5's corrected taxonomy.'

findings.finding: S3's file_impact list includes 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py' (reason: 'Add reconciler.backend key, default jira') and 'src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py' (reason: 'Static-frozenset validation of backend key') — but the S3 description body explicitly states: 'No src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py change and no new rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py — the earlier draft named both in error.' The contradictory file_impact entries will cause an implementer following the file list to open and edit the wrong files.
findings.location: S3 (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) file_impact list vs. description body
findings.suggested_fix: Replace S3 file_impact entries: remove `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/config.py` and `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/_config_schema.py`; add `src/rebar/_config_schema.py` with reason 'Add backend field to ReconcilerConfig + _as_choice coercer in reconciler coercer table'.

findings.finding: Parent AC-7 states 'adding a backend requires only a new adapters/<x>/ implementing the pinned interface — verified by the proof-of-seam FakeBackend passing the contract suite with no core edit,' but S3 (94f9) design requires editing _config_schema.py (a core app-level config file, not adapters/<x>/) to add the new key to the _as_choice static set for every new backend.
findings.location: child 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75 — Approach ('A second backend adds its key to both the _as_choice set and the registry'); parent Acceptance Criteria AC-7
findings.suggested_fix: Option A (update AC-7): Revise parent AC-7 to read 'adding a backend requires implementing adapters/<x>/ and adding its key to the _as_choice set in _config_schema.py and the registry.' Option B (redesign S3): derive the _as_choice allowed-set dynamically from the populated registry at config-load time rather than hardcoding it, so only the adapters/<x>/ addition is needed.

evidence: 1 criterion line(s) bundle multiple deliverables with 'and' (split so each is independently verifiable).
findings.finding: Acceptance-criteria / definition-of-done quality issues.
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to S4's scope and AC the migration of `test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85` (which accesses `outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` via the re-export that the neutrality gate requires removing): either update the test to access the constant through `adapters/jira/` or through the `Backend.outbound_mapper` protocol, so the full suite remains green when the re-export is dropped.
evidence: outbound_differ.py:63-69 re-exports `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE`, `_assignee_matches`, `_diff_fields`, `_extract_jira_field` from `outbound_fields` with `# noqa: F401` and `_diff_links`, `_existing_jira_links` from `outbound_links` — explicitly for the field-diff test suite
test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85: `assert 'session_log' not in outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` — accesses the re-exported name through `outbound_differ`
S4 neutrality-gate AC: 'None of the 12 rewired core modules import `adapters.jira.*` or the root vendor modules' — removing the re-export satisfies this gate but orphans the test
ABSENCE: S4 Scope, Testing, and AC sections make no mention of migrating this test or the re-exported symbols
findings.finding: S4 drops the `from rebar_reconciler.outbound_fields import _LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE, ...  # noqa: F401` re-export from `outbound_differ.py` to satisfy the neutrality gate, but one existing test (`test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85`) directly accesses `outbound_differ._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` via that re-export. S4's scope, AC, and testing sections do not mention updating this test, creating a hidden breakage that will fail the 'full suite green' AC without an obvious cause.
findings.location: S4 (`6673-7636-a116-4f90`) — Scope / Testing / Acceptance Criteria sections; confirmed at `tests/unit/rebar_reconciler/diffing/test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85` and `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/outbound_differ.py:63-69`
scenarios: S4 lands, `outbound_differ.py` drops the `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` re-export to pass the neutrality gate, `test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion` fails with `AttributeError: module 'outbound_differ' has no attribute '_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE'` — the full suite is not green and the cause is not covered by S4's testing plan
findings.suggested_fix: In S4's Scope section add: 'Update `test_outbound_differ_session_log_exclusion.py:85` to access `_LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE` (or its relocated equivalent) through the `OutboundMapper` protocol or directly from `adapters/jira/outbound_fields` — the re-export on `outbound_differ` is removed by the neutrality gate.' Add a corresponding item to S4's AC: 'No test accesses Jira-specific constants via `outbound_differ.*` re-exports — verified by `grep outbound_differ\._LOCAL_TO_JIRA_TYPE tests/` returning zero hits.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In dfb9, explicitly state what the S5 deliverable for outbound_comments.py is contingent on S4's outcome, OR update parent AC4 to reflect that outbound_comments.py's repointing may be fully discharged in S4 (and remove it as a definitive S5 deliverable).
evidence: Parent AC4: 'the two non-moved root modules that reference them by sibling filename (inbound_fields.py, outbound_comments.py) are repointed'
dfb9 Scope: 'Any outbound_comments.py / inbound_translate.py / reconcile_check.py adf/comment_limits loader refs not already eliminated by S4 — grep-confirmed at S5 time; repoint or drop'
6673 Context confirms outbound_comments.py as a vendor-importing site (lazy_load of adf.py + comment_limits.py) to be routed through Backend in S4
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: Parent AC4 definitively lists outbound_comments.py as one of the 'two non-moved root modules that reference them by sibling filename' that S5 must repoint. Child dfb9 makes this conditional: 'Any outbound_comments.py / inbound_translate.py / reconcile_check.py adf/comment_limits loader refs not already eliminated by S4 — grep-confirmed at S5 time; repoint or drop.' If S4 fully routes outbound_comments.py through the Backend port (eliminating its lazy_load calls), S5's repointing becomes a no-op and the parent's definitive AC4 deliverable is never explicitly verified as a distinct step. This is a partial-without-remainder gap: the remainder after S4 is unspecified.
findings.location: child dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9, Scope and Acceptance Criteria; parent AC4
scenarios: S4 fully rewires outbound_comments.py's lazy_load calls through the Backend mapper; S5 grep-confirms zero residual refs; the parent AC4's outbound_comments.py repoint is vacuously satisfied with no explicit verification
A reviewer checks S5's AC for the outbound_comments.py repoint and finds it absent because S4 already handled it — the epic AC is not clearly closed
findings.suggested_fix: Add to dfb9's AC3: 'outbound_comments.py's adf/comment_limits references are either (a) confirmed eliminated by S4's neutrality gate — in which case this AC is satisfied by S4 — or (b) repointed here; in either case grep confirms zero residual references.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Retitle 6673 to 'Route ADF-sizing/lossy-status/maps through Backend port (single-source the call sites; file move is S5)' and clarify AC2 to state 'single-sourced call path (core calls only the adapter mapper; the file move into adapters/jira/ completes in S5)'.
evidence: 6673 title: 'relocate ADF-sizing/lossy-status/maps into adapters/jira'
6673 Scope: 'Changes callers, not file locations'

6673 AC2: 'ADF size-fitting... single-sourced under adapters/jira/; grepping the core sites for those constructs returns zero hits (assertion is core-emptiness + single-sourcing, path-agnostic — the file move itself is S5)'
Parent decomposition: 'S4 = who-calls-what... S5 = where-files-live — disjoint edit-sets'
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: Child S4 (6673) uses the word 'relocate' in its title ('relocate ADF-sizing/lossy-status/maps into adapters/jira') and its AC2 asserts these constructs are 'single-sourced under adapters/jira/' — but S4's own scope says 'Changes callers, not file locations' and the physical files (adf.py, outbound_fields.py) remain at the package root until S5. After S4, adf.py still contains fit_text_to_adf_limit at the root path; 'single-sourced' describes call-site routing, not code deduplication. A completion verifier applying the literal AC2 text could reject S4 because the code is not yet deduplicated.
findings.location: child 6673-7636-a116-4f90, title and Acceptance Criteria AC2
scenarios: An implementer reads S4's title and physically moves adf.py in S4, breaking the atomicity S5 requires for the loader + patch migration
A completion verifier reads 'single-sourced under adapters/jira/' and rejects S4 because adf.py still exists at root
findings.suggested_fix: Change S4's AC2 to: 'ADF size-fitting, lossy status/parent mapping, the duplicated value-maps, and the outbound_links link constants are callable only through the Backend mapper from core (zero direct-import hits in core); the code may still reside at the package root until S5 moves it — the assertion is core-emptiness, not file location.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Explicitly state whether the `"jira"` literal appears in any serialized/persisted form (config, state cache, audit log) and, if so, add a migration/compatibility note for RemoteRef in the Design section.
evidence: Plan states RemoteRef replaces the 'jira' literal but scopes the discussion to in-code usage only
No mention of serialized forms, stored reconciler state, or audit-log output that might encode the bare 'jira' string
ABSENCE: no migration note for any persistence layer
findings.finding: `RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id}` replaces the hardcoded `"jira"` string literal, but the plan does not state whether this literal appears in any serialized/persisted artifact (config files, stored state, audit logs). If it does, the type substitution is a silent data-layer breaking change with no migration described.
findings.location: Design §'One new type RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} replaces the hardcoded "jira" literal'
scenarios: Reconciler state is persisted between runs (e.g. a cache or checkpoint file) encoding the string 'jira'; after the migration the reader expects a RemoteRef struct and silently misparses or crashes
Config validation that previously accepted `backend: jira` now expects a structured RemoteRef and rejects existing config files

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Reconcile `RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id}` against `be74`'s stated `{provider, external_key}` two-field model: either justify the three-field split to `be74`'s authors (e.g., `instance` separates multi-tenant Jira instances from the provider identity) and update `be74`'s AC to reflect it, or align `RemoteRef` to the two-field model before pinning it in ADR 0035 §(d).
evidence: Plan: 'One new type RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id} replaces the hardcoded "jira" literal'
be74 (ticket be74-7832-03a8-48ac): 'A {provider, external_key} binding abstraction replaces hardcoded jira_key, with Jira refactored onto it'

ABSENCE: No discussion in this epic or any child story of why RemoteRef carries three fields (vendor + instance + remote_id) vs. be74's two-field model, or how instance maps to be74's external_key vs. remote_id
findings.finding: The plan introduces `RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id}` as the identity type, but the downstream consumer epic `be74` (which this epic explicitly `blocks`) describes the desired binding abstraction as `{provider, external_key}` — a two-field model with no `instance` field. The three-field split is not discussed in the plan against `be74`'s stated model, leaving an unresolved coordination risk that could require a breaking change to `RemoteRef` when `be74` is implemented.
findings.location: Design section ('One new type RemoteRef{vendor, instance, remote_id}'); be74 description: 'a {provider, external_key} binding abstraction replaces hardcoded jira_key'
scenarios: be74 authors land the GitHub adapter assuming a two-field identity type and must retrofit a third field or introduce an adapter to bridge RemoteRef ↔ {provider, external_key}.
The binding store serializes RemoteRef with three fields; be74's binding migration must account for the extra field, adding unexpected scope.
findings.suggested_fix: If multi-instance Jira is the motivation for the `instance` field, document this explicitly in ADR 0035 §(d) and add a note to be74's description; if not, consider collapsing to `RemoteRef{provider, external_id}` matching be74's vocabulary.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to 6673's testing specification that any test invoking select_backend() uses _reset_registry_for_test (from S3) to isolate registry state, mirroring the isolation pattern established in S3.
evidence: 6673 Testing: 'the full reconcile suite (outbound + inbound passes over fixture stores) produces identical events/outcomes to pre-story main — the primary behavior-preserving guard'
6673 Testing: 'Neutrality gate: a test/grep asserts none of the 12 rewired core modules import ...'
94f9 introduces _reset_registry_for_test for exactly this isolation purpose
6673 Testing makes no mention of _reset_registry_for_test or registry isolation
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: S4 (6673) routes run_differs.py through select_backend(config).transport but S4's testing section does not specify how registry state is isolated between test runs (no mention of _reset_registry_for_test). Since select_backend() uses the module-level registry dict introduced in S3, S4's tests could interfere with each other or with S3's tests if run in the same process without explicit registry isolation.
findings.location: child 6673-7636-a116-4f90, Testing section
scenarios: S4's new test for run_differs.py's seam calls select_backend() which populates the registry as a side effect. A subsequent test in the same session that expects a clean registry state (e.g., the S3 teeth test) finds the registry pre-populated and the test fails or passes for the wrong reason.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to 6673 Testing: 'All test functions that call select_backend() wrap their body in _reset_registry_for_test() to ensure registry isolation across the test session.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] State the intended form of the 'neutrality gate' in AC3 (e.g., 'a pytest `test_core_neutrality.py` that asserts no `adapters.jira` in `sys.modules` after importing each of the 12 core sites', or 'a `forbidden-imports` entry in `.flake8`/`ruff`) so the executor has a concrete artifact to produce.
evidence: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the **12 rewired core sites**' — the word 'gate' is used but no artifact type (lint plugin, CI script, pytest conftest import assertion, etc.) is named
ABSENCE: neither the Design section nor any child-story description specifies the gate's implementation form
findings.finding: The 'neutrality gate' that enforces core non-importation of adapters.jira.* (AC3) is named but its form is unspecified — the plan does not say whether it is a lint rule, a CI script, a custom import checker, or a pytest marker, leaving the executor to invent the mechanism.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'enforced by a neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites'
scenarios: An executor implementing S4 must decide unilaterally what the gate artifact is; two different executors could produce incompatible mechanisms (e.g. a flake8 plugin vs. a shell grep) that later conflict when maintained
If the gate is implemented as a non-standard check, reviewers may not know how to verify it is actually enforced
findings.suggested_fix: Add one sentence to AC3: 'The gate is implemented as `tests/architecture/test_core_neutrality.py` — a pytest module that imports each of the 12 rewired core files in isolation and asserts `adapters.jira` does not appear in their transitive imports; this test is added to the default `make test` run in S4.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC bullet (or extend the S2 bullet) to cover the 'opt-in capability Protocols': enumerate them, state what 'opt-in' means structurally, and specify how the contract suite or a type-check gate verifies their presence.
evidence: 'Five role Protocols (`TicketTransport`, `OutboundMapper`, `InboundMapper`, `FieldSanitizer`, `IdentityConvention`) behind one `Backend` facade, + opt-in capability Protocols.'
S2 AC: 'the `Backend` Protocols, `RemoteRef`, and a contract suite passing against both `JiraBackend` and a test-only `FakeBackend` exist (S2).' — no mention of capability Protocols.
findings.finding: The Design section introduces 'opt-in capability Protocols' (beyond the five named role Protocols) as a deliverable, but no AC bullet covers their definition, presence, or contract-suite verification.
findings.location: Design section ('Five role Protocols … + opt-in capability Protocols') and AC section (S2 bullet covers 'Backend Protocols, RemoteRef, and a contract suite' but does not mention capability Protocols)
scenarios: An implementer may define capability Protocols in S2, but reviewers cannot verify them against any acceptance criterion, leaving their scope and correctness unverifiable at merge time.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Extend the S5 AC bullet to explicitly verify REMOVAL: add a predicate asserting the migrated files no longer exist at the package root (e.g., 'and the originals are deleted from the package root — verified by a CI file-existence gate or equivalent').
evidence: S5 AC: '`acli*`/`adf`/`outbound_fields`/`comment_limits` reside under `adapters/jira/`, the loader resolves the location-pinned modules there, the two non-moved root modules that reference them by sibling filename (`inbound_fields.py`, `outbound_comments.py`) are repointed, and all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims (S5).'

ABSENCE: no explicit predicate such as 'the files no longer exist at the package root' or 'a CI gate asserts absence from src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/{acli*,adf,outbound_fields,comment_limits}.py'.
findings.finding: The S5 AC verifies replacement (files 'reside under adapters/jira/') and absence of shims, but does not explicitly verify REMOVAL of the migrated modules from their original root location — a migration criterion should verify both sides.
findings.location: AC section, S5 bullet: '`acli*`/`adf`/`outbound_fields`/`comment_limits` reside under `adapters/jira/`…'
scenarios: A developer could satisfy the AC by copying modules to adapters/jira/ while leaving the originals at root, satisfying 'reside under adapters/jira/' but not achieving the actual migration goal.
findings.suggested_fix: Append to the S5 AC bullet: '…and the four modules no longer exist at the package root (verified by a path-assertion in CI or the test suite).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Amend the final AC bullet's verify step to reference BOTH the FakeBackend contract suite AND the S4 neutrality gate as joint prerequisites for the 'be74 is unblocked' claim (e.g., 'verified by: (a) FakeBackend passing the contract suite with no core edit, AND (b) the neutrality gate on the 12 core sites passing').
evidence: Final AC bullet verify step: 'verified by the proof-of-seam `FakeBackend` passing the contract suite with no core edit.'
S4 AC bullet: 'After S4 the backend-neutral core no longer imports `adapters.jira.*` or the root vendor modules — enforced by a neutrality gate on the 12 rewired core sites.'
A FakeBackend can pass the contract suite even if core still imports vendor modules directly — the FakeBackend test exercises the interface, not core neutrality.
findings.finding: The final AC bullet claims 'Epic be74 (first real second backend) is unblocked' and states this is 'verified by the proof-of-seam FakeBackend passing the contract suite with no core edit' — but the more critical predicate for unblocking (core neutrality: core no longer imports vendor modules) lives in a separate S4 AC bullet, making the stated verify step for the unblocking claim incomplete.
findings.location: AC section, final bullet ('Epic `be74` … is unblocked: adding a backend requires only a new `adapters/<x>/` implementing the pinned interface — verified by the proof-of-seam `FakeBackend` passing the contract suite with no core edit.')
scenarios: An implementer reads the final AC bullet in isolation and concludes that only the FakeBackend contract test needs to pass to claim epic be74 is unblocked, overlooking the S4 neutrality gate as a co-prerequisite.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace 'verified by the proof-of-seam `FakeBackend` passing the contract suite with no core edit' with 'verified jointly by: (a) `FakeBackend` passing the full contract suite with no core edit, and (b) the S4 neutrality gate asserting core imports zero vendor modules'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/back-out plan for S4 and S5 (e.g., keep re-export shims at old paths behind a deprecation flag until S5 is confirmed green, or document the exact revert commit sequence), satisfying T4's reversibility requirement beyond mere acknowledgement.
evidence: "no shims" is explicit but no back-out sequence is given
AC: 'all ~29 test patch strings are migrated with no re-export shims' — acknowledges the break, silent on reversal
S4 lands callers-change without physical relocation; if S5 is abandoned mid-flight, core imports nothing and no shim bridges the gap
findings.finding: S4 and S5 each make breaking changes (rewiring 12 call-sites; physically relocating 4 modules with no shims) but neither story provides a rollback/back-out plan. The plan acknowledges the breaks but T4 requires an explicit reversibility mechanism, not just acknowledgement.
findings.location: Child decomposition §S4 / §S5; AC line 'migrate the ~29 test patch strings atomically (no re-export shims)'
scenarios: S5 is partially applied (some modules moved, some not) and must be reverted — no shim means the window between S4-landed and S5-complete leaves core in a broken state with no fall-back
A hotfix is needed post-S5 that requires rolling back to the pre-epic state; no re-export shims means downstream consumers (outside the ~29 patched tests) have no bridge
findings.suggested_fix: For S5: introduce one-release re-export shims at the old root paths (e.g. `acli.py` → `from adapters.jira.acli import *`) gated on a `_COMPAT_SHIM` flag, remove them in a follow-up commit once the green suite is confirmed. For S4: document that reverting S4 is safe because the old files are still at the root — make this explicit in the story's rollback note.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a documentation task to S3 (or the epic-level AC) to update the configuration reference with the `reconciler.backend` key, its valid static values, and its default (`"jira"`).
evidence: S3 description: 'add reconciler.backend config (static-key validated)' — no mention of updating config reference docs
ADR 0035 §(d) covers the interface protocols but is not described as covering the config key schema
ABSENCE: no 'update config reference documentation' task in S3 or the AC
findings.finding: The new `config.reconciler.backend` config key is introduced in S3 but the plan contains no pointer to updating user-facing configuration documentation (reference docs, README config section, or the ADR's config schema appendix). Existing operators with undocumented config behavior will not know the key exists or what values are valid.
findings.location: Child decomposition §S3; AC 'add `reconciler.backend` config (static-key validated)'
scenarios: An operator upgrades rebar and has no config key set; they must discover by source inspection what the valid values are and what the default is
Existing config documentation shows the old config shape and becomes stale
findings.suggested_fix: Add a one-sentence schema entry to ADR 0035 §(d) or the existing config reference page: `reconciler.backend: <string, default 'jira'> — selects the vendor adapter; valid values: ['jira']`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit documentation-audit step to S5: grep all docs/ and README files for the old root-level module names (`acli`, `adf`, `outbound_fields`, `comment_limits`) and update or remove any stale references before the story is marked done.
evidence: '~29 test patch strings are migrated' — only test strings are listed, no other documentation scan is mentioned
ABSENCE: no 'scan docs for old import paths' step in S5 or epic AC
The plan explicitly says no re-export shims, so any stale doc reference cannot be self-healing
findings.finding: After S5, the original package-root paths for `acli*`, `adf`, `outbound_fields`, and `comment_limits` are permanently gone with no shims. The plan migrates ~29 test patch strings but does not mention scanning or updating any other documentation (README, architecture docs, existing ADR prose, or any onboarding guides) that may reference these modules by their old root-level paths, leaving stale references.
findings.location: Child decomposition §S5; AC 'physically relocate acli*/adf/outbound_fields/comment_limits into adapters/jira/'
scenarios: A developer follows an onboarding doc that says 'import from rebar_reconciler.adf' and gets an ImportError with no helpful message
ADR 0035 itself (written in Phase 1) may reference the old file layout; after S5 those references are stale

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Justify (in the ADR §(d) or S2 description) why opt-in capability Protocols use behavior-only detection rather than `@runtime_checkable` + `isinstance`, which is the established pattern in the same module cluster (`outbound_differ.py:228`, `inbound_differ.py:78`), or adopt `@runtime_checkable` for consistency.
evidence: Plan: 'a backend that does not implement SupportsLinks is never asked to sync links — observed via behavior, not via structural introspection'
Codebase: outbound_differ.py:228 and inbound_differ.py:78 both use @runtime_checkable Protocol + isinstance checks — the established in-repo capability-detection pattern
ABSENCE: No rationale in the plan for departing from the @runtime_checkable pattern already used in the same module cluster
findings.finding: The opt-in capability Protocols (SupportsLinks, SupportsComments, SupportsIncremental) are specified to be detected 'via behavior — not via structural introspection' rather than via @runtime_checkable + isinstance checks, yet the existing codebase already uses @runtime_checkable Protocols (outbound_differ.py:228, inbound_differ.py:78). The plan does not justify why the established in-codebase pattern is deliberately departed from for capability detection, leaving the novel approach unjustified against the prior art in the same repo.
findings.location: Design section ('Five role Protocols … + opt-in capability Protocols'); S2 (ce4d) Testing section: 'a capability Protocol's methods are exercised only when the backend advertises it (observed via behavior … not via structural introspection)'
scenarios: A future GitHub adapter implements SupportsComments but not SupportsLinks; without isinstance, the core must infer capability from try/except or duck-type calls, creating subtle failure modes that @runtime_checkable isinstance would make explicit.
A reviewer implementing S2 picks up @runtime_checkable (following existing patterns) while the plan intends behavior-only detection — inconsistency baked in from day one.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit 800-LOC cap verification AC item (pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py) to both ce4d (S2) and 6673 (S4).
evidence: Parent AC5: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap (.github/module-size-limit.txt) throughout'
94f9 AC: 'Every src/rebar/**/*.py stays within the 800-LOC cap — verified by pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py'
dfb9 AC: 'Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap'
ce4d AC: no 800-LOC check present
6673 AC: no 800-LOC check present
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: Parent AC5 ('Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC module-size cap throughout') is only explicitly verified in S3 (94f9) and S5 (dfb9). Children S2 (ce4d) and S4 (6673) have no 800-LOC cap check in their Acceptance Criteria, yet both stories introduce new code (Backend Protocols, JiraBackend, rewired core modules, new seams) that could push files over the cap.
findings.location: child ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798 Acceptance Criteria; child 6673-7636-a116-4f90 Acceptance Criteria
scenarios: S2 adds _backend.py and adapters/jira/backend.py; if either approaches 800 LOC no gate catches it at that story's landing
S4 rewires 12 core modules and adds new seams; a bloated rewired module is not caught at S4's gate
findings.suggested_fix: Add to ce4d AC and 6673 AC: '[ ] Every src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/*.py stays within the 800-LOC cap — verified by pytest tests/unit/test_module_size_contract.py, exit 0.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In 94f9, explicitly specify that _reset_registry_for_test must also handle sys.modules cache interaction (e.g., by re-importing adapters after reset to re-trigger @register, or by making the registry dict the sole source of truth and re-populating it from a saved snapshot rather than relying on re-import).
evidence: 94f9 Approach: 'select_backend lazily imports rebar_reconciler.adapters inside the function'
94f9 Approach: 'adapters/__init__.py is edited to from .jira import backend'
94f9 Testing teeth: 'via _reset_registry_for_test, register a FakeBackend under a test key... then confirm reverting the registration makes the same lookup raise'
94f9 Testing registry: 'register is idempotent (same key+factory = no-op; a conflicting factory for the same key raises)'
container pairing: parent + 6673-7636-a116-4f90/94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75/ce4d-c3ca-5412-4798/dfb9-d2d1-455d-48d9/ef33-eaf6-a545-4bfb
findings.finding: The lazy-import strategy in S3 (94f9) — 'select_backend lazily imports rebar_reconciler.adapters inside the function' — combined with Python's module-cache behavior creates an unspecified interaction with _reset_registry_for_test: clearing the registry dict does not un-import adapters/__init__.py or adapters/jira/backend.py from sys.modules, so the next call to select_backend() re-imports (no-op, cached) and re-triggers @register side-effects only if @register is idempotent. The S3 spec says 'register is idempotent (same key+factory = no-op)' — which would make the reset work correctly — but the teeth test (register a FakeBackend under a test key, revert, confirm lookup raises) requires the FakeBackend key to be absent after reset. If the lazy import immediately re-registers JiraBackend (idempotent, fine) but not FakeBackend (never imported), the teeth test should work. However, this interaction is never explicitly analyzed in S3 or S4, leaving an implicit correctness assumption unverified by spec.
findings.location: child 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75, Approach section (lazy import + _reset_registry_for_test) and Testing (teeth test)
scenarios: A test calls select_backend(), which caches the adapters import. _reset_registry_for_test clears the dict. The next select_backend() call re-imports adapters (no-op, cached module), which has already run 'from .jira import backend' — the @register side-effect ran at import time and is not re-run. The registry dict is now empty (from the reset) and JiraBackend is NOT re-registered. The lookup raises — unexpectedly failing a test that expected JiraBackend to be returned after reset.
findings.suggested_fix: Specify that _reset_registry_for_test saves and restores the full registry dict snapshot (not just clears it), so after exit the dict is exactly as before entry — no re-import dependency. This avoids the lazy-import/module-cache interaction entirely.

findings.finding: S3 describes the registry population mechanism as relying on 'the existing `adapters/__init__` that imports each shipped adapter,' but the actual `adapters/__init__.py` contains only a comment with no such auto-import. If an implementer reads 'existing' and does not add the auto-import trigger in S3, the registry will be empty at runtime and `select_backend()` will raise a key-not-found error even when `config.reconciler.backend == 'jira'` — breaking the default flow with no compile-time signal.
findings.location: S3 (ticket 94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) Approach section: 'select_backend imports that module (via the existing adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter)'
findings.suggested_fix: In S3's Approach and Scope sections, replace 'via the existing `adapters/__init__` that imports each shipped adapter' with 'by updating `adapters/__init__.py` to import each shipped adapter sub-package (e.g. `from . import jira`) — this auto-import is added in S3 and is the mechanism that triggers registration.' Add `src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/__init__.py` to S3's file_impact list.

findings.finding: S3 states that select_backend() populates the registry deterministically because 'Registration happens at import of adapters/jira/backend.py; select_backend imports that module (via the existing adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter).' However, the actual adapters/__init__.py in the repo contains only a comment and does NOT import adapters/jira/backend.py or any adapter submodule. The plan's stated mechanism for ensuring registry population — 'the existing adapters/__init__ that imports each shipped adapter' — does not exist. No plan artifact specifies who adds that import to adapters/__init__, when, or as part of which story.
findings.location: S3 (94f9-a9a0-7475-4e75) Approach section; src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/__init__.py
findings.suggested_fix: In S3 scope and file_impact, add: 'Update src/rebar/_engine/rebar_reconciler/adapters/__init__.py to import adapters.jira.backend (triggering JiraBackend registration at package import time).' Alternatively, have _backend_registry.py's select_backend() explicitly import the jira adapter module when the requested key is 'jira', rather than relying on a side-effect import chain.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
